I imagine reading the 686-word first-person reconstruction in a room of people deciding whether it demonstrates knowledge creation. If everyone already knows the historical structure it draws on, then the reconstruction can feel packed with insight while mainly rearranging familiar material. In that setting, it is a weak test. To make it a stronger test, the target theory would have to be hidden in advance, so the reconstruction could not merely ride on known scaffolding.

Now suppose someone presents the result as evidence for progress “not through scaling, but through architecture.” That slogan cannot be taken at face value. Even if the immediate gain comes from architecture, the architecture may still lean on priors that were produced by scaling somewhere else, earlier in the pipeline. So the contrast is not clean. Architecture may be the visible mechanism of success while inherited scaled priors are the invisible subsidy.

A dispute follows about standards. One side invokes Deutschian expectations: good explanations should be hard to vary. But that standard, applied too early, would dismiss many promising hypotheses. Productive early-stage ideas are often loose, adjustable, and underconstrained. Only later, after mathematical consolidation, do they become rigid in the way that “hard to vary” demands. So if the audience insists on rigidity at the start, it will systematically underrate exploratory work, even when that work is on the path to stronger explanation.

The social scene matters because the audience has a boutique complexion and ignored the booger-like projections. Whatever those projections are, they are visible enough to be ignored rather than unseen. Their neglect suggests selectivity guided by taste, status, or surface preference rather than by a neutral search for explanatory traction. The audience is not merely evaluating theories; it is filtering what counts as presentable.

In the same room, shyness makes the joyful monitor seem like an abstract treasury. The monitor is joyful, but shyness transforms that immediacy into distance and symbol: a treasury, but abstract. This means the evaluators are not encountering the scene plainly. Their own affect changes what appears concrete into something remote and conceptual.

Put together, the world is one where apparent insight can be confounded with dependence on prior structure, architectural success can secretly rely on scaled priors, strict explanatory standards can be mistimed, and social-aesthetic filters can hide salient features. The shy observer, facing a selective audience and a monitor transmuted into abstraction, is especially likely to misread what is actually being produced. In such a world, claims of genuine knowledge creation are hard to assess unless theory targets are concealed beforehand and unless one separates true explanatory gain from borrowed structure, inherited priors, premature rigor demands, and the audience’s selective inattention.
